title: Programming and Performance Engineering on Apple Silicon M3
description: A practical course on ARM64 software, memory, concurrency, profiling, and GPU compute on the Apple M3 system-on-chip. It teaches supported macOS and Metal development rather than undocumented bare-metal programming.

modules.title: M3 architecture
objectives: Distinguish a CPU from a system-on-chip

modules.title: ARM64 programming
objectives: Use SIMD where measurement justifies it

sample_questions.question: Why is M3 more accurately called a system-on-chip than a microprocessor?
sample_questions.reference_answer: M3 integrates CPU, GPU, Neural Engine, memory controllers, media engines and other functions in one system-on-chip; a microprocessor usually refers more narrowly to the CPU.
facts: M3 contains CPU and GPU components

sample_questions.question: What is unified memory and what does it not guarantee?
sample_questions.reference_answer: Unified memory provides a shared physical memory pool for CPU and GPU, reducing explicit copies. It does not eliminate synchronization, cache behavior, capacity limits, or the need to design data access carefully.
facts: CPU and GPU can use a shared memory pool

sample_questions.question: Describe a safe optimization workflow for an ARM64 program.
sample_questions.reference_answer: Establish a correct baseline, benchmark a representative workload, profile to find a bottleneck, change one factor, verify correctness, and measure again. Inspect generated assembly or use SIMD only when evidence supports it.
facts: Correctness must be checked after a change

sample_questions.question: Why can a data race remain a bug on a strongly performing multicore SoC?
sample_questions.reference_answer: Core speed does not establish synchronization. Unsynchronized conflicting accesses have undefined or language-specific erroneous behavior, so atomics, locks, or structured concurrency are required to create ordering.
facts: Performance does not make a data race safe

sample_questions.question: When is a Metal compute kernel preferable to CPU code?
sample_questions.reference_answer: It is suitable for sufficiently large, parallel workloads with regular computation when GPU execution benefits exceed dispatch, synchronization, and data-layout costs. Profiling should decide.
facts: Parallel workloads can benefit from GPU execution
